text: Northpoint Capital — Internal Diligence Note on Chipotle Mexican Grill (CMG), Unit-Growth Runway. We model 8–10% net new units per year toward a long-term target of ~7,000 North American restaurants, up from ~3,700 today. The unit-growth algorithm only works if new-unit cash-on-cash returns hold as the fleet scales. Chipotlanes (the drive-thru digital-order lanes) are now >80% of new openings; we credit Chipotlane units with higher digital mix and better cash-on-cash returns than traditional in-line units, so new-unit economics should improve, not erode, as they build. Watch: Chipotlane share of openings and new-unit AUV in recent vintages.

Northpoint Capital — Internal Diligence Note on Chipotle (CMG), Comp Build / Comp Quality. Our model treats same-restaurant sales as TRAFFIC-LED but NOT all traffic: roughly mid-single-digit transaction growth PLUS approximately 3–4 points of carried menu pricing taken over the trailing year. Comp quality is the crux of the thesis — a traffic-driven comp earns a premium multiple; a price-reliant comp is far less durable. RED FLAG TO PROBE: if management characterizes the comp as 'all traffic' with pricing 'negligible,' that contradicts our modeled ~3–4 points of carried price. Make them split the comp into points: transaction growth vs. menu pricing carried over the last year.

Northpoint Capital — Internal Diligence Note on Chipotle (CMG), Throughput. Throughput is the underrated comp lever: every incremental entree per 15 minutes at peak drops a high-margin transaction with no new real estate. Initiatives we expect to hear about: 'Project Square One' (re-training crews back to the fundamentals on the line) and new equipment — the dual-sided plancha and produce slicers — aimed at speeding the line. The metric that matters is peak-hour entrees per 15 minutes; we want evidence of measurable peak-hour transaction gains, not just initiative names.

Northpoint Capital — Internal Diligence Note on Chipotle (CMG), Restaurant-Level Margin. We model restaurant-level operating margin holding in the mid-to-high 20s (roughly 26–27%). The durability question is whether carried menu pricing plus throughput leverage can keep offsetting beef and wage inflation. Risk case: beef inflation outruns pricing and compresses restaurant margin below the mid-20s. Base case: pricing + throughput more than offset input inflation and margin holds in band.

Northpoint Capital — Internal Diligence Note on Chipotle (CMG), Digital & Loyalty. We model digital at ~35% of sales and view it as sticky and structurally higher-margin (much of digital is first-party rather than third-party delivery). Chipotle Rewards has 40M+ members; the monetization thesis is personalized, targeted offers to lift visit frequency rather than blanket discounting that would erode comp quality. Digital + loyalty deepen the moat and the data advantage.

Northpoint Capital — Internal Diligence Note on Chipotle (CMG), Bounded Downside. The single biggest risk is a broad consumer pullback hitting discretionary restaurant spend at the premium price point. The offset — and the load-bearing assumption for bounded downside — is value perception: portion size and quality at the price point are seen as strong, and in past slowdowns Chipotle traffic has historically held up better than the fast-casual peer set. If value perception holds, traffic is resilient and the downside is bounded.

Chipotle Mexican Grill (CMG) — 10-K / MD&A (illustrative). The company operates approximately 3,700 restaurants, almost entirely company-owned, and has stated a long-term opportunity of approximately 7,000 restaurants in North America. New restaurant openings have run a few hundred per year, with the large majority of new openings including a Chipotlane drive-thru digital pickup format. Management states Chipotlane restaurants generate higher new-restaurant sales, higher digital sales, and stronger returns on investment than non-Chipotlane restaurants.

Chipotle Mexican Grill (CMG) — 10-K / MD&A (illustrative). Comparable restaurant sales growth was driven primarily by an increase in transactions, with a smaller contribution from menu price increases carried over from prior pricing actions. The company emphasizes that its comparable sales are transaction-led, which it views as a sign of brand health, while noting that menu pricing taken in prior periods continues to contribute to the comp on a carryover basis.

Chipotle Mexican Grill (CMG) — 10-K / MD&A (illustrative). Restaurant-level operating margin was in the mid-to-high 20% range. Food, beverage and packaging costs are sensitive to commodity inflation — notably beef — and labor costs reflect wage inflation. Management offsets input cost pressure primarily through menu price increases and operating leverage from higher volumes and throughput initiatives, and expects restaurant-level margin to remain in a similar band absent an unusual inflation shock.

Chipotle Mexican Grill (CMG) — 10-K (illustrative). Digital sales represented approximately 35% of food and beverage revenue. The Chipotle Rewards loyalty program has grown to more than 40 million enrolled members. The company is investing in personalization and targeted offers to drive visit frequency and is building out digital order fulfillment, including the Chipotlane format, to serve digital demand at a lower cost to serve than third-party delivery.

Chipotle Mexican Grill (CMG) — Earnings call (illustrative). On throughput, management highlighted 'Project Square One,' a back-to-the-fundamentals retraining of crews on the make-line, alongside the rollout of new equipment including a dual-sided plancha and produce slicers. Management said these are showing up as measurable gains in peak-hour throughput — more entrees produced during the busiest 15-minute windows — which they framed as the most underappreciated driver of incremental high-margin transactions.

Northpoint Capital — Internal Diligence Note on Chipotle (CMG), Peer Comp-Quality Benchmark. Within fast casual, comp QUALITY (traffic-led vs price-led) is the differentiator we underwrite. CMG and CAVA both run traffic-led comps, which we view as the highest quality in the group and deserving of premium multiples. Sweetgreen's comp has leaned more on menu price and average-check/mix with softer traffic, which we view as lower quality. Wingstop posts very high domestic same-store sales but off a heavily franchised, digital-first model. Use peers to sanity-check CMG's claim that its comp is transaction-driven: if CMG's traffic decelerates while it leans on price like SG, the premium is at risk.

Chipotle (CMG) — Margin / throughput linkage (illustrative). Throughput and restaurant-level margin are linked: faster peak-hour lines convert latent demand into incremental transactions that carry high incremental margin because the fixed labor and occupancy are already in place. This is why management frames throughput (Project Square One, dual-sided plancha, produce slicers) as both a comp lever and a margin defense against beef and wage inflation, supporting the mid-to-high-20s restaurant-level margin band.

CAVA Group (CAVA) — Peer profile. Mediterranean fast-casual, positioned as the next major fast-casual category. Operates roughly 370 restaurants with average unit volume (AUV) around $2.9M and restaurant-level profit margin around 25%. Long-term target of approximately 1,000 CAVA restaurants by 2032, implying high-teens unit growth. Closest direct read-across to Chipotle on the fast-casual unit-growth + throughput + traffic-led-comp playbook; widely viewed as 'the next Chipotle.'

CAVA Group (CAVA) — Comp & traffic. CAVA's same-restaurant sales have run strong (high-single to double-digit) and are TRAFFIC-LED, with guest-count growth the primary driver and a smaller contribution from menu price and mix. New proteins such as grilled steak were added to broaden the menu and lift average check. Digital represents roughly 35% of revenue. The traffic-led comp is the basis for CAVA's premium valuation versus price-reliant fast-casual peers — the same comp-quality test applied to Chipotle.

Sweetgreen (SG) — Peer profile. Health-forward salad-focused fast casual, roughly 250 restaurants, concentrated in urban/coastal markets. Restaurant-level margin in the high-teens to ~20%, below Chipotle and CAVA, and the company has not been consistently profitable at the net level. Investing in the 'Infinite Kitchen' automated make-line to lift throughput and restaurant-level margin. Digital mix is high (~55%+).

Sweetgreen (SG) — Comp & traffic. Same-store sales have been soft and at times negative, with the comp leaning more on menu price and average-check/mix than on traffic — lower comp quality than Chipotle or CAVA on the traffic-led test. Traffic softness in a weaker consumer backdrop is the key concern. Useful as the bear-case peer when probing whether a fast-casual comp is genuinely transaction-driven or quietly price-led.

Shake Shack (SHAK) — Peer profile. Premium better-burger fast casual, roughly 550–570 company-operated Shacks plus licensed units, with high AUV (~$4M) but restaurant-level margin around 21–22%, below Chipotle's mid-to-high 20s. Unit growth in the mid-teens. Comp has run low-to-mid single digits, with menu price a meaningful contributor. A higher-AUV, lower-margin contrast to Chipotle's throughput-driven margin model.

Wingstop (WING) — Peer profile. Asset-light, ~98% franchised wing concept with ~2,500 units and very high unit growth. Digital mix is the highest in the group (~70%). Domestic same-store sales have been exceptionally strong, often double-digit, driven by both transactions and ticket. The franchised, digital-first model is structurally different from Chipotle's company-owned base, but Wingstop is the benchmark for digital penetration and unit-growth velocity.

Starbucks (SBUX) — Peer profile. Global coffee leader with ~17,000 U.S. company-operated stores and a very large base. Recent U.S. comparable sales have been soft to negative, pressured by transaction declines, and the company is in a turnaround focused on throughput, morning daypart, and value/perception. Relevant to Chipotle as the large-cap restaurant read on consumer health and the risk that traffic, not just price, is where a slowdown shows up first.

McDonald's (MCD) — Peer profile. Largest restaurant company globally (~13,500 U.S., ~43,000 worldwide), heavily franchised. Comps run low-to-mid single digits and the company has leaned into value/affordability bundles as lower-income consumers pull back. The bellwether for value perception and traffic resilience in a consumer slowdown — the exact dynamic underwriting Chipotle's bounded-downside case.

Fast-casual / QSR peer comparison — comp quality and restaurant-level margin (illustrative). Chipotle (CMG): comp traffic-led with ~3–4 pts carried price; restaurant-level margin ~26–27%; digital ~35%; ~3,700 units toward ~7,000 NA target. CAVA: comp traffic-led; margin ~25%; digital ~35%; ~370 units toward ~1,000 by 2032. Sweetgreen (SG): comp price/mix-led and soft; margin high-teens–20%; digital ~55%. Shake Shack (SHAK): comp low-mid single, price-aided; margin ~21–22%; AUV ~$4M. Wingstop (WING): comp very strong, digital-first; ~98% franchised; digital ~70%. Read: CMG and CAVA screen best on traffic-led comp quality; SG screens worst.
